revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | Image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | Image Size = | Image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:qq]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
{{Normdaten}} {{DEFAULTSORT:Yamaga Lantern Festival}} [[Category:Festivals in Kumamoto Prefecture]] [[Category:Yamaga]] [[Category:Emperor Keikō]] ==See Also==

{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | Image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | Image Size = | Image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:qq]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].

== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:qq]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].

{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | Image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | Image Size = | Image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:11]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].

{{イベントインフォメーション | イベント名称 = 山鹿灯籠まつり | 英文表記 = | 画像 = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = 山鹿燈籠 | 種類 = [[祭り]] | 通称 = | 正式名称 = | 旧名称 = | 開催時期 = [[8月]] | 初回開催 = | 最終開催 = | 開催時間 = | 会場 = [[熊本県]][[山鹿市]] | 主催 = | 共催 = | 後援 = | 協賛 = | 企画制作 = | 協力 = | 運営 = | プロデューサー = | 出展数 = | 来場者数 = | 会場アクセス名 = <!--複数会場がある場合、主な会場名を記載--> | 最寄駅 = | 直通バス = | 駐車場 = <!--駐車場の有無--> | URL = | 特記事項 = }} '''山鹿灯籠まつり'''（やまがとうろうまつり）は、[[熊本県]][[山鹿市]]、[[山鹿温泉]]にある[[大宮神社 (山鹿市)|大宮神社]]の[[祭り]]であり、その諸行事は毎年8月15日から17日にかけて行われている。[[民謡]]「よへほ節」のメロディーにのって、浴衣姿の女性が、金・銀の和紙作られた金[[灯籠]]（かなとうろう）を頭にのせ、市内を踊り歩く。[[熊本市]]の[[藤崎八旛宮秋季例大祭|藤崎八旛宮の秋季例大祭]]、[[八代市]]の[[八代神社]]の妙見祭とならび、[[熊本県]]を代表する年中行事のひとつとして数えられている。
== 祭りの由来 == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|山鹿市の金剛乗寺]] 灯籠まつりは[[景行天皇]]伝説に起源があるとも室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=山鹿市教育委員会 |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref>。 * 景行天皇伝説とは、景行天皇が九州を巡幸し菊池川を遡って上陸しようとしたところ（現山鹿大橋付近）、濃霧が立ちこめ一行は進路を見失ってしまっていたため、山鹿の里人が[[松明]]を灯して一行を導いた<ref name="matsumoto" />。以後、住民は行在所跡（後に大宮神社が建てられた地）に景行天皇を祀って毎年松明を奉納したという伝説である<ref name="matsumoto" />。 * 室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。[[室町時代|室町]]中期、山鹿の温泉が枯れてしまったとき、宥明法印（山鹿金剛乗寺）が祈祷によってふたたび温泉を湧き出させた。この宥明法印の没後、その追善のために灯籠が奉納された、とする説などがある。
戦国時代になり灯籠まつりは一度途絶えたが、[[文禄]]年間に再びまつりは復活したといわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。文化・文政期には多くの住民が灯籠作りに携わり最盛期となったが、幕末には各地で戦乱が続き灯籠作りも衰退してしまった<ref name="matsumoto" />。
明治時代になると木村仙太郎らの優れた灯籠師が現れて、灯籠まつりも復活し大宮神社に多くの灯籠が奉納された<ref name="matsumoto" />。
灯籠踊りは[[昭和30年]]ごろに[[藤間流]]の藤間勘太女がお座敷用の踊りとして考案した振り付けを屋外用に改変したものである<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author1 = [[三隅治雄]] |title = 日本の民謡と舞踊 |date = 1990 |publisher = 大阪書籍 |isbn = 4754810856 |ref = harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>。
== スケジュール == === 奉納灯籠 === * [[大宮神社 (山鹿市)|大宮神社]]に奉納される[[灯籠]]（'''奉納灯籠'''）は、各町ごとに灯籠師（とうろうし）に製作を依頼する。完成した奉納灯籠は、[[8月15日]]、それぞれ街角に展示し、地元の人々や観客に披露される。 * そして各町での展示を終えた奉納灯籠は、[[8月17日]]の午前0時、各町から大宮神社まで、「ハーイ、トウロウ、ハーイ、トウロウ」のかけ声にのせて運ばれていく。これを'''「上がり灯籠」'''という。大宮神社に奉納された[[灯籠]]は、神前でお祓いをうけて献灯されてから、大宮公園にならべて展示される。 * かつては、明け方ちかくの午前4時、神社での展示を終えた[[灯籠]]を各町に持ち帰る'''「下がり灯籠」'''が行われていたが、現在は大宮公園での展示終了後、神社の灯籠殿に収蔵され、翌年の祭りまで引き続き展示されるようになっている（その後、一部の灯籠は[[山鹿灯籠民藝館]]などで展示される）。
=== 灯籠踊り === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|千人灯籠踊り]] * 灯籠踊りは、大宮神社および参道、おまつり広場、山鹿小学校グラウンドで見学することができる。 * 祭り初日の[[8月15日]]、午後6時30分から大宮神社境内で、山鹿灯籠踊り保存会による'''「奉納灯籠踊り」'''が奉納される。その後、参道やおまつり広場で、各種団体によって灯籠踊りが披露される。おまつり広場での踊りは夜遅く午後11時過ぎまでつづく。 * なお、[[8月15日]]には午後8時から[[菊池川]]河川敷で[[花火]]大会が催されている。 * [[8月16日]]、午前10時から大宮神社で例祭献幣式が行われる。この日の灯籠踊りも夕方からおまつり広場などで披露されるが、午後6時45分からと9時30分からの二回、山鹿小学校グラウンドで行われる'''「千人灯籠踊り」'''がこの祭りの最大のクライマックスである。頭上に金灯籠（かなとうろう）をのせた浴衣姿の女性、千人による優雅な踊りは圧巻であり、ゆったりした「よへほ節」が会場に流れるなか、薄暗闇に千の灯が浮かび、櫓を中心にして渦のように流れ、揺らめく。（途切れないカメラのフラッシュを除けば）とても幻想的な光景である。 * なお、[[8月16日]]の午後8時30分から、[[菊池川]]河川敷で、[[景行天皇]]奉迎儀式と、それに続き松明行列が行われる。「祭りの由来」を参照。

== 祭りの由来 == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|山鹿市の金剛乗寺]] 灯籠まつりは[[景行天皇]]伝説に起源があるとも室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=山鹿市教育委員会 |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref>。 * 景行天皇伝説とは、景行天皇が九州を巡幸し菊池川を遡って上陸しようとしたところ（現山鹿大橋付近）、濃霧が立ちこめ一行は進路を見失ってしまっていたため、山鹿の里人が[[松明]]を灯して一行を導いた<ref name="matsumoto" />。以後、住民は行在所跡（後に大宮神社が建てられた地）に景行天皇を祀って毎年松明を奉納したという伝説である<ref name="matsumoto" />。 * 室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。[[室町時代|室町]]中期、山鹿の温泉が枯れてしまったとき、宥明法印（山鹿金剛乗寺）が祈祷によってふたたび温泉を湧き出させた。この宥明法印の没後、その追善のために灯籠が奉納された、とする説などがある。
戦国時代になり灯籠まつりは一度途絶えたが、[[文禄]]年間に再びまつりは復活したといわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。文化・文政期には多くの住民が灯籠作りに携わり最盛期となったが、幕末には各地で戦乱が続き灯籠作りも衰退してしまった<ref name="matsumoto" />。
明治時代になると木村仙太郎らの優れた灯籠師が現れて、灯籠まつりも復活し大宮神社に多くの灯籠が奉納された<ref name="matsumoto" />。
灯籠踊りは[[昭和30年]]ごろに[[藤間流]]の藤間勘太女がお座敷用の踊りとして考案した振り付けを屋外用に改変したものである<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author1 = [[三隅治雄]] |title = 日本の民謡と舞踊 |date = 1990 |publisher = 大阪書籍 |isbn = 4754810856 |ref = harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>。
== スケジュール == === 奉納灯籠 === * [[大宮神社 (山鹿市)|大宮神社]]に奉納される[[灯籠]]（'''奉納灯籠'''）は、各町ごとに灯籠師（とうろうし）に製作を依頼する。完成した奉納灯籠は、[[8月15日]]、それぞれ街角に展示し、地元の人々や観客に披露される。 * そして各町での展示を終えた奉納灯籠は、[[8月17日]]の午前0時、各町から大宮神社まで、「ハーイ、トウロウ、ハーイ、トウロウ」のかけ声にのせて運ばれていく。これを'''「上がり灯籠」'''という。大宮神社に奉納された[[灯籠]]は、神前でお祓いをうけて献灯されてから、大宮公園にならべて展示される。 * かつては、明け方ちかくの午前4時、神社での展示を終えた[[灯籠]]を各町に持ち帰る'''「下がり灯籠」'''が行われていたが、現在は大宮公園での展示終了後、神社の灯籠殿に収蔵され、翌年の祭りまで引き続き展示されるようになっている（その後、一部の灯籠は[[山鹿灯籠民芸館]]などで展示される）。
=== 灯籠踊り === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|千人灯籠踊り]] * 灯籠踊りは、大宮神社および参道、おまつり広場、山鹿小学校グラウンドで見学することができる。 * 祭り初日の[[8月15日]]、午後6時30分から大宮神社境内で、山鹿灯籠踊り保存会による'''「奉納灯籠踊り」'''が奉納される。その後、参道やおまつり広場で、各種団体によって灯籠踊りが披露される。おまつり広場での踊りは夜遅く午後11時過ぎまでつづく。 * なお、[[8月15日]]には午後8時から[[菊池川]]河川敷で[[花火]]大会が催されている。 * [[8月16日]]、午前10時から大宮神社で例祭献幣式が行われる。この日の灯籠踊りも夕方からおまつり広場などで披露されるが、午後6時45分からと9時30分からの二回、山鹿小学校グラウンドで行われる'''「千人灯籠踊り」'''がこの祭りの最大のクライマックスである。頭上に金灯籠（かなとうろう）をのせた浴衣姿の女性、千人による優雅な踊りは圧巻であり、ゆったりした「よへほ節」が会場に流れるなか、薄暗闇に千の灯が浮かび、櫓を中心にして渦のように流れ、揺らめく。（途切れないカメラのフラッシュを除けば）とても幻想的な光景である。 * なお、[[8月16日]]の午後8時30分から、[[菊池川]]河川敷で、[[景行天皇]]奉迎儀式と、それに続き松明行列が行われる。「祭りの由来」を参照。

{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 2]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:11]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].

revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:11]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.

revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:11]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.

Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" />[[Category:Completion tier 3]][[Category:Tier 2]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]][[Category:11]] The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].

revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
{{Normdaten}}
==See Also==
** {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en}} <!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> ** <!--Yamaga Lantern Festival-->
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> in {{ill|Russia|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival-->.
* {{ill|Spring Festivals|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> * {{ill|Autumn Festivals|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> * {{ill|Winter Festivals|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> * {{ill|Tanabata|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> * {{ill|Community building|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> * {{ill|Homecoming|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en}}<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival-->

{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
{{Normdaten}}

revision.text:
{{イベントインフォメーション | イベント名称 = 山鹿灯籠まつり | 英文表記 = | 画像 = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = 山鹿燈籠 | 種類 = [[祭り]] | 通称 = | 正式名称 = | 旧名称 = | 開催時期 = [[8月]] | 初回開催 = | 最終開催 = | 開催時間 = | 会場 = [[熊本県]][[山鹿市]] | 主催 = | 共催 = | 後援 = | 協賛 = | 企画制作 = | 協力 = | 運営 = | プロデューサー = | 出展数 = | 来場者数 = | 会場アクセス名 = <!--複数会場がある場合、主な会場名を記載--> | 最寄駅 = | 直通バス = | 駐車場 = <!--駐車場の有無--> | URL = | 特記事項 = }} '''山鹿灯籠まつり'''（やまがとうろうまつり）は、[[熊本県]][[山鹿市]]、[[山鹿温泉]]にある[[大宮神社 (山鹿市)|大宮神社]]の[[祭り]]であり、その諸行事は毎年8月15日から17日にかけて行われている。[[民謡]]「よへほ節」のメロディーにのって、浴衣姿の女性が、金・銀の和紙作られた金[[灯籠]]（かなとうろう）を頭にのせ、市内を踊り歩く。[[熊本市]]の[[藤崎八旛宮秋季例大祭|藤崎八旛宮の秋季例大祭]]、[[八代市]]の[[八代神社]]の妙見祭とならび、[[熊本県]]を代表する年中行事のひとつとして数えられている。
== 祭りの由来 == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|山鹿市の金剛乗寺]] 灯籠まつりは[[景行天皇]]伝説に起源があるとも室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=山鹿市教育委員会 |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref>。 * 景行天皇伝説とは、景行天皇が九州を巡幸し菊池川を遡って上陸しようとしたところ（現山鹿大橋付近）、濃霧が立ちこめ一行は進路を見失ってしまっていたため、山鹿の里人が[[松明]]を灯して一行を導いた<ref name="matsumoto" />。以後、住民は行在所跡（後に大宮神社が建てられた地）に景行天皇を祀って毎年松明を奉納したという伝説である<ref name="matsumoto" />。 * 室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。[[室町時代|室町]]中期、山鹿の温泉が枯れてしまったとき、宥明法印（山鹿金剛乗寺）が祈祷によってふたたび温泉を湧き出させた。この宥明法印の没後、その追善のために灯籠が奉納された、とする説などがある。
戦国時代になり灯籠まつりは一度途絶えたが、[[文禄]]年間に再びまつりは復活したといわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。文化・文政期には多くの住民が灯籠作りに携わり最盛期となったが、幕末には各地で戦乱が続き灯籠作りも衰退してしまった<ref name="matsumoto" />。
明治時代になると木村仙太郎らの優れた灯籠師が現れて、灯籠まつりも復活し大宮神社に多くの灯籠が奉納された<ref name="matsumoto" />。
灯籠踊りは[[昭和30年]]ごろに[[藤間流]]の藤間勘太女がお座敷用の踊りとして考案した振り付けを屋外用に改変したものである<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author1 = [[三隅治雄]] |title = 日本の民謡と舞踊 |date = 1990 |publisher = 大阪書籍 |isbn = 4754810856 |ref = harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>。
== スケジュール == === 奉納灯籠 === * [[大宮神社 (山鹿市)|大宮神社]]に奉納される[[灯籠]]（'''奉納灯籠'''）は、各町ごとに灯籠師（とうろうし）に製作を依頼する。完成した奉納灯籠は、[[8月15日]]、それぞれ街角に展示し、地元の人々や観客に披露される。 * そして各町での展示を終えた奉納灯籠は、[[8月17日]]の午前0時、各町から大宮神社まで、「ハーイ、トウロウ、ハーイ、トウロウ」のかけ声にのせて運ばれていく。これを'''「上がり灯籠」'''という。大宮神社に奉納された[[灯籠]]は、神前でお祓いをうけて献灯されてから、大宮公園にならべて展示される。 * かつては、明け方ちかくの午前4時、神社での展示を終えた[[灯籠]]を各町に持ち帰る'''「下がり灯籠」'''が行われていたが、現在は大宮公園での展示終了後、神社の灯籠殿に収蔵され、翌年の祭りまで引き続き展示されるようになっている（その後、一部の灯籠は[[山鹿灯籠民芸館]]などで展示される）。
=== 灯籠踊り === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|千人灯籠踊り]] * 灯籠踊りは、大宮神社および参道、おまつり広場、山鹿小学校グラウンドで見学することができる。 * 祭り初日の[[8月15日]]、午後6時30分から大宮神社境内で、山鹿灯籠踊り保存会による'''「奉納灯籠踊り」'''が奉納される。その後、参道やおまつり広場で、各種団体によって灯籠踊りが披露される。おまつり広場での踊りは夜遅く午後11時過ぎまでつづく。 * なお、[[8月15日]]には午後8時から[[菊池川]]河川敷で[[花火]]大会が催されている。 * [[8月16日]]、午前10時から大宮神社で例祭献幣式が行われる。この日の灯籠踊りも夕方からおまつり広場などで披露されるが、午後6時45分からと9時30分からの二回、山鹿小学校グラウンドで行われる'''「千人灯籠踊り」'''がこの祭りの最大のクライマックスである。頭上に金灯籠（かなとうろう）をのせた浴衣姿の女性、千人による優雅な踊りは圧巻であり、ゆったりした「よへほ節」が会場に流れるなか、薄暗闇に千の灯が浮かび、櫓を中心にして渦のように流れ、揺らめく。（途切れないカメラのフラッシュを除けば）とても幻想的な光景である。 * なお、[[8月16日]]の午後8時30分から、[[菊池川]]河川敷で、[[景行天皇]]奉迎儀式と、それに続き松明行列が行われる。「祭りの由来」を参照。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『観光と旅43 郷土資料事典 熊本県』 [[人文社]] 1990年 * 『都道府県別 祭礼行事 熊本県』 [[おうふう]] 1997年 * 『日本の祭り 九州・沖縄2』週刊朝日百科No.11 [[朝日新聞社]] 2004年8月15日号
{{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:やまかとうろうまつり}} [[Category:熊本県の祭り]] [[Category:山鹿市]] [[Category:景行天皇]]

revision.text:
{{イベントインフォメーション | イベント名称 = 山鹿灯籠まつり | 英文表記 = | 画像 = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | 画像サイズ = | 画像説明 = 山鹿燈籠 | 種類 = [[祭り]] | 通称 = | 正式名称 = | 旧名称 = | 開催時期 = [[8月]] | 初回開催 = | 最終開催 = | 開催時間 = | 会場 = [[熊本県]][[山鹿市]] | 主催 = | 共催 = | 後援 = | 協賛 = | 企画制作 = | 協力 = | 運営 = | プロデューサー = | 出展数 = | 来場者数 = | 会場アクセス名 = <!--複数会場がある場合、主な会場名を記載--> | 最寄駅 = | 直通バス = | 駐車場 = <!--駐車場の有無--> | URL = | 特記事項 = }} '''山鹿灯籠まつり'''（やまがとうろうまつり）は、[[熊本県]][[山鹿市]]、[[山鹿温泉]]にある[[大宮神社 (山鹿市)|大宮神社]]の[[祭り]]であり、その諸行事は毎年8月15日から17日にかけて行われている。[[民謡]]「よへほ節」のメロディーにのって、浴衣姿の女性が、金・銀の和紙作られた金[[灯籠]]（かなとうろう）を頭にのせ、市内を踊り歩く。[[熊本市]]の[[藤崎八旛宮秋季例大祭|藤崎八旛宮の秋季例大祭]]、[[八代市]]の[[八代神社]]の妙見祭とならび、[[熊本県]]を代表する年中行事のひとつとして数えられている。
== 祭りの由来 == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|山鹿市の金剛乗寺]] 灯籠まつりは[[景行天皇]]伝説に起源があるとも室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|和書|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=山鹿市教育委員会 |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref>。 * 景行天皇伝説とは、景行天皇が九州を巡幸し菊池川を遡って上陸しようとしたところ（現山鹿大橋付近）、濃霧が立ちこめ一行は進路を見失ってしまっていたため、山鹿の里人が[[松明]]を灯して一行を導いた<ref name="matsumoto" />。以後、住民は行在所跡（後に大宮神社が建てられた地）に景行天皇を祀って毎年松明を奉納したという伝説である<ref name="matsumoto" />。 * 室町時代に始まった行事ともいわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。[[室町時代|室町]]中期、山鹿の温泉が枯れてしまったとき、宥明法印（山鹿金剛乗寺）が祈祷によってふたたび温泉を湧き出させた。この宥明法印の没後、その追善のために灯籠が奉納された、とする説などがある。
戦国時代になり灯籠まつりは一度途絶えたが、[[文禄]]年間に再びまつりは復活したといわれている<ref name="matsumoto" />。文化・文政期には多くの住民が灯籠作りに携わり最盛期となったが、幕末には各地で戦乱が続き灯籠作りも衰退してしまった<ref name="matsumoto" />。
明治時代になると木村仙太郎らの優れた灯籠師が現れて、灯籠まつりも復活し大宮神社に多くの灯籠が奉納された<ref name="matsumoto" />。
灯籠踊りは[[昭和30年]]ごろに[[藤間流]]の藤間勘太女がお座敷用の踊りとして考案した振り付けを屋外用に改変したものである<ref>{{Cite book |和書 |author1 = [[三隅治雄]] |title = 日本の民謡と舞踊 |date = 1990 |publisher = 大阪書籍 |isbn = 4754810856 |ref = harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>。
== スケジュール == === 奉納灯籠 === * [[大宮神社 (山鹿市)|大宮神社]]に奉納される[[灯籠]]（'''奉納灯籠'''）は、各町ごとに灯籠師（とうろうし）に製作を依頼する。完成した奉納灯籠は、[[8月15日]]、それぞれ街角に展示し、地元の人々や観客に披露される。 * そして各町での展示を終えた奉納灯籠は、[[8月17日]]の午前0時、各町から大宮神社まで、「ハーイ、トウロウ、ハーイ、トウロウ」のかけ声にのせて運ばれていく。これを'''「上がり灯籠」'''という。大宮神社に奉納された[[灯籠]]は、神前でお祓いをうけて献灯されてから、大宮公園にならべて展示される。 * かつては、明け方ちかくの午前4時、神社での展示を終えた[[灯籠]]を各町に持ち帰る'''「下がり灯籠」'''が行われていたが、現在は大宮公園での展示終了後、神社の灯籠殿に収蔵され、翌年の祭りまで引き続き展示されるようになっている（その後、一部の灯籠は[[山鹿灯籠民芸館]]などで展示される）。
=== 灯籠踊り === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|千人灯籠踊り]] * 灯籠踊りは、大宮神社および参道、おまつり広場、山鹿小学校グラウンドで見学することができる。 * 祭り初日の[[8月15日]]、午後6時30分から大宮神社境内で、山鹿灯籠踊り保存会による'''「奉納灯籠踊り」'''が奉納される。その後、参道やおまつり広場で、各種団体によって灯籠踊りが披露される。おまつり広場での踊りは夜遅く午後11時過ぎまでつづく。 * なお、[[8月15日]]には午後8時から[[菊池川]]河川敷で[[花火]]大会が催されている。 * [[8月16日]]、午前10時から大宮神社で例祭献幣式が行われる。この日の灯籠踊りも夕方からおまつり広場などで披露されるが、午後6時45分からと9時30分からの二回、山鹿小学校グラウンドで行われる'''「千人灯籠踊り」'''がこの祭りの最大のクライマックスである。頭上に金灯籠（かなとうろう）をのせた浴衣姿の女性、千人による優雅な踊りは圧巻であり、ゆったりした「よへほ節」が会場に流れるなか、薄暗闇に千の灯が浮かび、櫓を中心にして渦のように流れ、揺らめく。（途切れないカメラのフラッシュを除けば）とても幻想的な光景である。 * なお、[[8月16日]]の午後8時30分から、[[菊池川]]河川敷で、[[景行天皇]]奉迎儀式と、それに続き松明行列が行われる。「祭りの由来」を参照。
== 脚注 == {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 『観光と旅43 郷土資料事典 熊本県』 [[人文社]] 1990年 * 『都道府県別 祭礼行事 熊本県』 [[おうふう]] 1997年 * 『日本の祭り 九州・沖縄2』週刊朝日百科No.11 [[朝日新聞社]] 2004年8月15日号
{{Normdaten}} {{デフォルトソート:やまかとうろうまつり}} [[Category:熊本県の祭り]] [[Category:山鹿市]] [[Category:景行天皇]]

{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
{{Normdaten}}
==See Also==
** {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en}} **
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft}}.

revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==
** {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|qq=}} **
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}
*
