[[sn:Yamaga Lantern Festival]] [[ja:山鹿灯籠まつり]] [[ja:宮崎県]] [[ja:サバントゥイ]] [[ja:ヴォルガ川]] [[ja:ロシア]] [[ja:春祭り]] [[ja:秋祭り]] [[ja:冬祭り]] [[ja:七夕]] [[ja:地域おこし]] [[ja:帰省]] [[ja:夏休み]] [[zh:%E5%A4%8F%E7%A5%AD]] [[ja:夏祭り]]
[[sn:amaga Lantern Festival]]
<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> [[Category:Yamaga, Kumamoto]] [[Category:景行天皇]] [[Category:Festivals of Kumamoto prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Yamaga Lantern Festival}} [[zh:山鹿燈籠祭]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Summer festivals in Japan}} [[zh:夏祭]]

{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.

* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

<!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--><!--Yamaga Lantern Festival--> [[Category:Yamaga, Kumamoto]] [[Category:Emperor Keikō]] [[Category:Festivals of Kumamoto prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Yamaga Lantern Festival}} [[zh:山鹿燈籠祭]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Summer festivals in Japan}} [[zh:夏祭]]

revision.comment: Bot: remove deleted category Japanese Culture
revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==
** {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|qq=}} **
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==

* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[Bunroku]] era. By the [[Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.

== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==

* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.

The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>

=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].

revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==
** {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|qq=}} **
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}
*

revision.comment: Bot: convert local link → enwiki interwiki
revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[Meiji period]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==
** {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|qq=}} **
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==

* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==

* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==

* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}

revision.comment: Merge [[Category:Festivals of Japan]] into [[Category:Festivals in Japan]] via bot
revision.text:
{{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==
** {{ill|Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Miyakonojo Rokugatsudo Okage Festival|qq=}} **
* {{ill|Sabantuy|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sabantuy|qq=}} – A summer festival held along the middle reaches of the {{ill|Volga River|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Volga River|qq=}} in {{ill|Russia|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Russia|qq=}}.
* {{ill|Haru Matsuri|lt=Haru Matsuri|lt=Spring Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Spring Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Aki Matsuri|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Autumn Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Fuyu Matsuri|lt=Winter Festivals|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Winter Festivals|qq=}} * {{ill|Tanabata|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tanabata|qq=}} * {{ill|Community building|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Community building|qq=}} * {{ill|Homecoming|en|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Homecoming|qq=}} * {{ill|Summer Vacation|en|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Summer Vacation|qq=}}
*

Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].

[[Category:Emperor Keikō]] [[Category:Festivals in Kumamoto Prefecture]] <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Yamaga Lantern Festival}} [[zh:山鹿燈籠祭]] <!--zhwiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{Commons category|Summer festivals in Japan}} [[zh:夏祭]]

{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11471245]] Wikidata name: Yamaga Lantern Festival [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:24, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{EventInfo | Event Name = Yamaga Lantern Festival | English Name = | image = File:Yamaga-Lantern.JPG | image Size = | image Description = Yamaga Lantern | Type = [[:en:Festival|Festival]] | Nickname = | Official Name = | Former Name = | Dates = [[August]] | First Held = | Last Held = | Time = | Venue = [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], [[Yamaga City]] | Organizer = | Co-organizer = | Supporters = | Sponsors = | Planning = | Cooperation = | Operations = | Producer = | Exhibits = | Visitors = | Main Access Venue = | Nearest Station = | Direct Bus = | Parking = | Website = | Special Notes = }} '''Yamaga Lantern Festival''' (やまがとうろうまつり) is a [[:en:Festival|Festival]] held annually at [[Ōmiya Shrine (Yamaga City)|Ōmiya Shrine]] in [[Yamaga City]], [[:en:Kumamoto Prefecture|Kumamoto Prefecture]], centered around the [[Yamaga Onsen]]. Events take place from August 15 to 17 each year. Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].

Female participants, dressed in summer yukata, gracefully balance gold lanterns (金灯籠) made of gold and silver Japanese paper on their heads as they dance to the melody of the traditional folk song "Yoheho Bushi." Alongside the [[Fujisaki Hachimangu Autumn Festival]] in [[:en:Kumamoto City|Kumamoto City]] and the Myōken Festival at [[Yatsushiro Shrine]] in [[Yatsushiro City]], it is one of the most iconic festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture.
== Origin of the Festival == [[File:Yamaga-Kongoujouji-Ishimon.JPG|thumb|Kongōjō Temple in Yamaga]] The origins of the Lantern Festival are tied to the legendary tale of [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] and are also believed to date back to the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]].<ref name="matsumoto">{{Cite web|lang=ja|url=https://www.city.yamaga.kumamoto.jp/www/contents/1281667313180/files/012.PDF |title=近代の山鹿の偉人たちシリーズ12 松本清記 |publisher=Yamaga City Board of Education |accessdate=2019-12-13}}</ref> * According to legend, during [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]]'s imperial tour of Kyushu, dense fog enveloped his entourage as they ascended the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]]. Residents of Yamaga lit torches to guide them safely to shore near present-day Yamaga Bridge. The emperor was later enshrined at the site of his temporary residence, where Ōmiya Shrine now stands, and annual torch offerings were made in his honor.<ref name="matsumoto" /> * Another account ties the festival to the Muromachi period, when the Yamaga Onsen dried up. A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
The festival was temporarily discontinued during the [[:en:Sengoku period|Sengoku period]], but it was revived during the [[:en:Bunroku|Bunroku]] era. By the [[:en:Bunka|Bunka]] and [[:en:Bunsei|Bunsei]] periods, lantern-making had flourished, involving many locals. However, production declined due to conflicts during the late [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].<ref name="matsumoto" />
In the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji era]], skilled lantern artisans like Kimura Sentarō emerged, rejuvenating the festival with numerous lantern offerings to Ōmiya Shrine.<ref name="matsumoto" /> The lantern dance choreography was developed in the [[Shōwa 30s]] by Kantajo Fujima of the [[Fujima School]] as a performance for indoor stages and was later adapted for outdoor settings.<ref>{{Cite book |lang=ja |author1=Haruo Misumi |title=日本の民謡と舞踊 |date=1990 |publisher=Osaka Shoseki |isbn=4754810856 |ref=harv }}pp.148-152.</ref>
== Schedule == === Lantern Offerings === * The lanterns offered to [[Ōmiya Shrine]] (奉納灯籠) are crafted by artisans commissioned by various neighborhoods. These lanterns are displayed publicly on August 15 in their respective areas before being carried to the shrine in a procession known as "Ascending Lanterns" (上がり灯籠) at midnight on August 17. At the shrine, the lanterns are purified and lit before being exhibited in Ōmiya Park. * Historically, a "Descending Lanterns" (下がり灯籠) procession would return the lanterns to their neighborhoods at 4 a.m. However, lanterns are now stored in the shrine's hall after the festival for continued display until the following year.
=== Lantern Dance === [[File:千人燈籠舞.jpg|thumb|The Thousand Lantern Dance]] * The lantern dance can be observed at Ōmiya Shrine, along the approach, and in festival squares. * On the evening of August 15, the Yamaga Lantern Dance Preservation Society performs a ceremonial dance at the shrine. Afterward, various groups perform dances at festival squares until 11 p.m. * A fireworks display is held at the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]] embankment at 8 p.m. on August 15. * On August 16, the main rituals begin with a ceremonial offering at Ōmiya Shrine at 10 a.m. Evening performances include the Thousand Lantern Dance (千人灯籠踊り), a breathtaking highlight involving a thousand yukata-clad women balancing lanterns as they dance gracefully to the serene "Yoheho Bushi." The dance, performed at 6:45 p.m. and 9:30 p.m. at Yamaga Elementary School grounds, creates an enchanting atmosphere of floating lights and rhythmic elegance. * Later that evening, the festival reenacts the legendary torchlight procession and welcoming ceremony for [[:en:Emperor Keikō|Emperor Keikō]] along the [[:en:Kikuchi River|Kikuchi River]].
== References == {{Reflist}}
== Bibliography == * "Kumamoto Prefecture Tourism and Travel Guide," Jinbunsha, 1990. * "Festivals of Kumamoto Prefecture," Ōfū, 1997. * "Japanese Festivals: Kyushu & Okinawa Volume 2," Asahi Encyclopedia, No. 11, August 15, 2004.
==See Also==

A monk named Yūmyō Hōin from Kongōjō Temple performed rituals that restored the hot springs. After his passing, lanterns were offered in his memory.<ref name="matsumoto" />
